05 React Server Components — The Biggest Paradigm Shift Since Hooks

← Back to lessons

RSC changes what "React component" means. Some components run only on the server. Their JavaScript never ships to the browser. This isn't SSR — it's a fundamentally new architecture. Let's build the mental model.

The Mental Model: Two Computers

Before RSC, all React components ultimately ran in the browser. Even with SSR, the same code ran on the server (for initial HTML) and then again in the browser (hydration). Same code, two environments.

RSC splits components into two categories that run on different machines:

Server Components (default)
// Runs ONLY on the server.
// JS never ships to client.
// Can access DB, filesystem, env vars.
// Cannot use hooks, event handlers, 
// or browser APIs.

async function ArticlePage({ id }) {
  // Direct database query — no API needed
  const article = await db.article.findUnique({
    where: { id }
  });

  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{article.title}</h1>
      <p>{article.body}</p>
      <LikeButton articleId={id} />
    </article>
  );
}
Client Components ("use client")
// Runs in the browser.
// JS ships to client bundle.
// Can use hooks, state, event handlers,
// and browser APIs.

'use client';

function LikeButton({ articleId }) {
  const [liked, setLiked] = useState(false);

  async function handleClick() {
    setLiked(true);
    await fetch(`/api/like/${articleId}`, {
      method: 'POST'
    });
  }

  return (
    <button onClick={handleClick}>
      {liked ? 'Liked!' : 'Like'}
    </button>
  );
}

The newsroom analogy: Think of Server Components as the research department. They have access to all the archives, databases, and confidential sources (server resources). They produce content (HTML/data) that gets published. Client Components are the interactive elements — the comment section, the poll widget, the share button. The research doesn't ship to the reader's doorstep; only the published article and the interactive widgets do.

What RSC Is Not (Debunking Confusion)

RSC is not SSR

SSR renders components to HTML on the server, then ships all the JavaScript to the browser for hydration. The same code runs in both places.

RSC renders server components to a serialized format (not HTML) that includes pre-rendered output AND placeholders for client components. Server component code never reaches the browser. Client components hydrate normally.

// SSR: Server renders to HTML, client re-runs everything
Server: ArticlePage() → HTML string
Client: ArticlePage() → hydrate (re-run SAME code, attach event listeners)

// RSC: Server renders server components, client only runs client components
Server: ArticlePage() → serialized tree (includes rendered HTML + client component markers)
Client: Only LikeButton() runs → hydrate just the interactive parts

RSC is not "components that render on the server"

SSR components also render on the server. The key difference: RSC components produce zero client-side JavaScript. A Server Component that imports a 200KB markdown parser sends zero bytes of that parser to the browser. The parsing happens on the server, and only the rendered HTML reaches the client.

Bundle size implications: Imagine a data journalism article that uses D3, lodash, a date formatting library, and a CSV parser to process data. As a Server Component, all of that processing happens on the server. The client receives pre-rendered HTML. The JavaScript bundle for that page: ~0KB from those libraries. This is transformative for performance.

The Serialization Boundary

The boundary between server and client components is the serialization boundary. Data crossing this boundary must be serializable — it gets turned into a wire format (React's RSC payload).

What can cross the boundary (as props)

  • Primitives: strings, numbers, booleans, null, undefined
  • Plain objects and arrays (of serializable values)
  • Date objects (serialized as ISO strings)
  • Server Actions (special function references)
  • JSX (pre-rendered by the server)

What cannot cross the boundary

  • Functions (except Server Actions)
  • Classes / class instances
  • DOM nodes / Refs
  • Symbols
  • Closures
// Server Component passing data to Client Component
async function Dashboard() {
  const data = await getAnalytics();  // Server-side fetch

  return (
    <div>
      <h1>Analytics Dashboard</h1>
      {/* These props must be serializable */}
      <InteractiveChart
        data={data.chartPoints}      // ✓ array of objects
        title="Monthly Visitors"      // ✓ string
        onHover={handleHover}         // ✗ FAILS — functions can't serialize
      />
    </div>
  );
}

The most common RSC error: "Functions cannot be passed directly to Client Components unless you explicitly expose them with 'use server'." This means you tried to pass a regular function from a server component to a client component. The fix: either make it a Server Action (with "use server") or define the function inside the client component.

Composition Patterns: Server Wrapping Client

Server Components can render Client Components. Client Components can render Server Components only if passed as children or props.

// PATTERN: Server Component wraps Client Component
// (This is the most common pattern)
async function ArticlePage({ id }) {
  const article = await db.article.findUnique({ where: { id } });

  return (
    <article>
      <h1>{article.title}</h1>
      <ArticleContent content={article.body} />
      {/* Client component — only this ships JS */}
      <CommentSection articleId={id} />
    </article>
  );
}

// PATTERN: Client Component receives Server Component as children
'use client';
function Sidebar({ children }) {
  const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(true);

  return (
    <aside className={isOpen ? 'open' : 'closed'}>
      <button onClick={() => setIsOpen(!isOpen)}>Toggle</button>
      {isOpen && children} {/* children can be Server Components! */}
    </aside>
  );
}

// In a Server Component:
function Layout() {
  return (
    <Sidebar>
      <ServerRenderedNav />  {/* Pre-rendered on server, passed as children */}
    </Sidebar>
  );
}

The children slot is the escape hatch. A Client Component can't import and render a Server Component directly (it would need to run on the server). But it can receive pre-rendered Server Component output via the children prop. The parent (a Server Component) does the rendering, and the Client Component just places the result in its tree. This is composition at its finest.

Server Actions: Functions That Cross the Boundary

Server Actions are the mechanism for Client Components to call server-side code. They're functions marked with "use server" that get serialized into RPC-style endpoints.

// Server Action — defined in a server file or inline with "use server"
'use server';

export async function publishArticle(formData: FormData) {
  const title = formData.get('title') as string;
  const body = formData.get('body') as string;

  // Direct database access — this runs on the server
  const article = await db.article.create({
    data: { title, body, published: true },
  });

  // Revalidate the articles page cache
  revalidatePath('/articles');

  // Redirect to the new article
  redirect(`/articles/${article.id}`);
}

// Client Component using the Server Action
'use client';

function PublishForm() {
  return (
    <form action={publishArticle}>
      <input name="title" required />
      <textarea name="body" required />
      <button type="submit">Publish</button>
    </form>
  );
}

Server Actions work with progressive enhancement. The form above works even without JavaScript. The browser submits the form as a standard POST request, the server action processes it, and the browser receives the redirect. When JS is available, React intercepts the submission and handles it with a fetch call, preserving client-side state. This is the best of both worlds.

The Complete Mental Model

Think of your application as two programs that communicate over a wire:

  1. The Server Program: Has access to databases, environment variables, file system. Runs Server Components. Produces serialized UI descriptions. Handles Server Actions.
  2. The Client Program: Runs in the browser. Hydrates Client Components. Handles user interactions. Calls Server Actions when it needs the server to do something.

The "wire" between them is the RSC payload — a serialized format that includes rendered Server Component output and markers for where Client Components should be placed.

// What flows over the wire (simplified RSC payload):
[
  "div",
  null,
  [
    ["h1", null, "Breaking: Budget Bill Passes"],       // Pre-rendered server output
    ["p", null, "By Trevor Brown · March 18, 2026"],    // Pre-rendered server output
    ["$client", "CommentSection", { articleId: "42" }]  // Client component marker
  ]
]

Check Your Understanding

1. What's the key difference between SSR and RSC?

SSR renders components to HTML on the server but ships all the component JavaScript to the browser for hydration. RSC keeps server component code entirely on the server — zero bytes of server component JS reach the browser. Only client component JS ships.

2. Can a Client Component import and render a Server Component?

Not directly. A Client Component runs in the browser and can't execute server-side code. However, a Client Component can receive pre-rendered Server Component output via the children prop or other JSX props. The parent Server Component does the rendering; the Client Component just places the result.

3. Why can't you pass a regular function from a Server Component to a Client Component?

Functions can't be serialized over the wire. The RSC payload is JSON-like — it can carry strings, numbers, objects, and arrays, but not function closures. Server Actions are the exception: they're special functions marked with "use server" that React serializes as RPC endpoint references.

4. A Server Component imports a 500KB charting library. How much of that library reaches the browser?

Zero bytes. The library runs on the server to produce the rendered output (SVG, HTML, etc.). Only the output reaches the browser. This is one of RSC's biggest wins for bundle size — heavy processing libraries stay on the server.

5. When would you add "use client" to a component?

When the component needs browser-only features: useState, useEffect, event handlers (onClick, onChange), browser APIs (window, document, localStorage), or third-party libraries that use any of these. The "use client" directive marks the boundary — this component and everything it imports becomes part of the client bundle.

Exercise: Draw the Boundary

For each component tree below, identify which components should be Server Components and which need "use client":

  1. A blog post page: Header (static), Article body (fetched from DB), Share buttons (onClick), Comment form (useState + form submission), Related articles sidebar (fetched from DB)
  2. A dashboard: Stats cards (DB query), Interactive chart (D3 + mouse events), Data table (sortable columns with onClick), Export CSV button (triggers download)
  3. An e-commerce product page: Product info (DB), Image carousel (useState for current slide), Add to cart button (onClick + animation), Reviews list (DB), Review form (useState + validation)

The goal: minimize the "use client" boundary. Push it as deep as possible so the maximum amount of code stays on the server.
